title: G7 leaders agree on new Russia sanctions as Ukraine ceasefire talks stall
raw_news:
G7 foreign ministers have reached a consensus on a fresh package of economic sanctions targeting Russia's energy sector and financial institutions, as diplomatic efforts to broker a ceasefire in Ukraine continued to falter, officials said Thursday.

The agreement, reached after two days of talks in Rome, would impose additional restrictions on Russian oil exports and block several state-owned banks from accessing Western payment systems. The measures are expected to be formally announced by G7 heads of government at a summit next month.

Canadian Foreign Affairs Minister Melanie Joly, speaking at a joint news conference after the meetings concluded, called the package 'the most comprehensive set of economic measures we have taken since the beginning of the conflict.' She said Canada had pushed for broader restrictions on Russia's shadow fleet of oil tankers, which has been used to circumvent existing price caps.

The sanctions announcement came as talks mediated by Turkey between Russian and Ukrainian negotiators in Istanbul ended without a breakthrough for the third consecutive round. Ukrainian officials said Russia had refused to withdraw from any of the four regions it claims to have annexed, a precondition Kyiv has insisted on before agreeing to any formal ceasefire arrangement.

Russian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Maria Zakharova dismissed the new sanctions as 'economic warfare dressed up in diplomatic language,' and said Moscow would respond with counter-measures targeting European agricultural exports.

European Union officials indicated the bloc would move in lockstep with the G7 package, though Hungary's government signalled it could seek carve-outs protecting its existing energy import agreements with Russia. EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas warned that any member state exemptions would 'undermine the credibility of the entire framework.'

Markets responded cautiously to the announcement. Brent crude rose modestly as traders assessed the potential impact on global oil supplies. European natural gas futures were little changed, reflecting expectations that the new measures would not immediately disrupt flows through existing contracts.

The Biden administration, whose successor has taken a markedly different tone toward Moscow, was not part of the G7 discussions but sent an observer. A White House spokesperson said the administration would review the package and indicated it was 'separately pursuing diplomatic engagement' with Russia.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky welcomed the sanctions announcement but said it was 'not enough on its own' and renewed calls for advanced long-range missile systems and additional air defence batteries. He said Ukraine had intercepted more than 60 drones targeting Kharkiv and Odesa in the past 48 hours.

Human rights organizations urged G7 members to move faster on asset seizure legislation that would allow the roughly 300 billion dollars in frozen Russian central bank reserves to be transferred to Ukraine for reconstruction. Several G7 governments have been cautious about that step, citing legal and financial stability concerns.

title: Bank of Canada holds rate steady, citing trade uncertainty and sticky inflation
raw_news:
The Bank of Canada held its benchmark interest rate at 2.75 per cent on Wednesday, pausing a rate-cutting cycle it began last year as policymakers weighed persistent services inflation against growing risks from a global trade slowdown driven by U.S. tariff policy.

Governor Tiff Macklem, at a news conference following the rate decision, acknowledged that the central bank faced an unusually complicated set of crosscurrents. 'We are balancing two significant forces pulling in opposite directions,' he said. 'Inflation in some services categories remains above target, while the external environment has become more uncertain in ways that could weigh on growth.'

The decision was in line with the expectations of most economists. The Bank's quarterly Monetary Policy Report, released alongside the rate announcement, trimmed its 2025 GDP growth forecast to 1.4 per cent from the 1.8 per cent projected in January, citing the drag from new U.S. tariffs on Canadian steel, aluminum and automotive parts.

Core inflation, which strips out volatile food and energy prices, eased to 2.9 per cent in February from 3.1 per cent in January but remained above the Bank's 2 per cent target. Shelter costs, which include rent and mortgage carrying costs, continued to be the largest single contributor to above-target readings.

Derek Holt, head of capital markets economics at Scotiabank, said the pause made sense given the data. 'The Bank is buying time to see how trade tensions resolve and whether the services inflation stickiness is temporary or embedded,' he said. 'Another cut in June remains possible if the next two CPI prints come in soft.'

Financial markets priced in roughly a 40 per cent probability of a 25-basis-point cut at the June meeting, down from about 55 per cent before the decision. The Canadian dollar strengthened slightly against the U.S. dollar following the announcement.

Macklem said the Bank was monitoring the labour market closely, noting that job creation had slowed in the first quarter. The unemployment rate edged up to 6.7 per cent in February, its highest level in more than two years, driven partly by a rise in youth unemployment and a slowdown in hiring in the construction and manufacturing sectors.

On the housing market, Macklem said the data suggested that lower borrowing costs implemented since last summer had begun to stimulate activity, with resale home prices in Toronto and Vancouver rising modestly in recent months. He cautioned, however, that a sustained housing recovery could complicate the inflation outlook.

Business groups urged the Bank to be ready to cut again quickly if trade conditions deteriorated further. The Canadian Chamber of Commerce said many of its members reported pulling back on investment and hiring decisions until they had more clarity on the tariff situation. 'Uncertainty is itself a drag on the economy,' said president and CEO Candace Shaw.

The Bank's next scheduled rate decision is May 28. Macklem declined to provide explicit forward guidance, saying future decisions would be data-dependent.

title: AI firms back international safety standards as governments race to regulate
raw_news:
Leading artificial intelligence companies including OpenAI and Anthropic have expressed conditional support for a framework of international safety standards being developed by a newly formed network of national AI safety institutes, even as they pushed back against provisions they say would slow deployment of beneficial technologies.

The AI Safety Institute Network, which includes bodies from Canada, the United Kingdom, the United States, Japan and the European Union, published a draft framework Tuesday outlining common evaluation requirements for advanced AI models before they are released to the public. The document proposes that models above a certain capability threshold be subject to mandatory third-party safety testing, with results disclosed to regulators.

In a joint statement, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind and Meta AI said they 'broadly support the goal of building public trust through rigorous evaluation' but called for clearer definitions of which systems would be subject to mandatory review and warned against evaluation processes that could delay time-sensitive research applications.

Canada's AI Safety Institute, established last year and headquartered in Ottawa, played a central role in drafting the capability thresholds section of the framework. Institute director Dr. Fiona Chau said the draft was designed to be technology-neutral and would apply equally to systems developed by large corporations and those produced by open-source research communities.

'We are not trying to pick winners or impose a particular design philosophy,' Chau said in an interview. 'What we are trying to ensure is that when a system reaches a level where it could pose meaningful risks, there is a credible, independent process for understanding those risks before wide deployment.'

The framework drew immediate criticism from some researchers who argued it was too permissive. Stuart Russell, a professor of computer science at the University of California, Berkeley, said the proposed capability thresholds were too high and would exclude systems that were already exhibiting concerning behaviours in automated agentic settings.

Others expressed concern about competitive dynamics. Several smaller AI developers warned that mandatory third-party evaluation could create barriers to entry that favoured large incumbents with dedicated safety teams and resources to manage lengthy regulatory processes.

The European Union, which has already enacted its AI Act, indicated it would seek to align the new international framework with its existing domestic legislation. EU officials acknowledged there were some definitional differences that would need to be reconciled.

In Canada, the federal government said it planned to introduce amendments to Bill C-27, its proposed AI legislation, to incorporate the international framework standards once finalized. Privacy and technology advocacy groups were divided on the approach, with some welcoming stronger guardrails and others warning that complex compliance requirements could disadvantage Canadian startups.

The draft framework is open for public comment for 60 days. A final version is expected to be presented to G7 leaders at the summit in July.

title: James Webb telescope finds chemical hints of life on distant planet, scientists urge caution
raw_news:
Astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope have detected chemical signatures in the atmosphere of a planet orbiting a distant star that could, under some scenarios, indicate biological processes — a finding that has generated intense excitement and equally intense calls for caution within the scientific community.

The research, published Thursday in the journal Nature Astronomy, reports the detection of dimethyl sulfide in the atmosphere of K2-18b, a planet roughly 2.6 times the size of Earth located about 124 light years away in the constellation Leo. On Earth, dimethyl sulfide is produced almost exclusively by marine phytoplankton and other ocean microorganisms. The researchers say the concentration detected is far higher than could be explained by any known abiotic chemical process.

'This is not a claim that we have found life,' said Dr. Nikku Madhusudhan of Cambridge University, who led the study. 'What we can say is that we have found a chemical signal that, if it were found on Earth, we would interpret as having a biological origin. The question is whether that interpretation holds for a world very different from our own.'

K2-18b orbits in the habitable zone of its host star, a red dwarf, meaning temperatures could theoretically allow liquid water to exist on its surface. The planet belongs to a class called 'Hycean worlds' — a hypothetical category of ocean-covered planets with hydrogen-rich atmospheres that some scientists believe could support microbial life.

The detection required combining data from more than 20 hours of observations taken by Webb's Near Infrared Imager and Slitless Spectrograph instrument. The team used machine learning tools to disentangle the faint atmospheric signal from the much brighter light of the host star.

Independent researchers welcomed the findings but urged the public not to jump to conclusions. Sara Seager, an exoplanet scientist at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology who was not involved in the study, said several alternative explanations remained viable. 'There are chemical pathways that do not require life that we may not yet fully understand for hydrogen-dominated atmospheres,' she said. 'We need more data and we need to model this more carefully.'

The Canadian Space Agency contributed funding to Webb through its partnership in the mission and has several researchers involved in ongoing atmospheric characterization campaigns. CSA president Lisa Campbell called the detection 'a remarkable demonstration of what this telescope can do' and said Canada was committed to supporting further observations.

The research team is requesting additional Webb observation time to look for corroborating biosignatures and to rule out alternative explanations. Results from those follow-up observations are expected within 18 months.

The finding reignited a broader scientific debate about how much evidence would be required before the research community could make a credible claim of having detected extraterrestrial life. Many scientists said consensus would require multiple independent confirmations using different instruments and methods.

title: Toronto food bank sees record visits as working Canadians struggle with cost of living
raw_news:
The Daily Bread Food Bank in Toronto set a new record for monthly visits in February, with more than 280,000 individual visits logged — a figure that would have been unimaginable five years ago and one that its leadership says reflects a crisis among working Canadians that is no longer hidden.

Nearly half of the people who visited a Daily Bread location last month reported being employed, according to data the organization shared exclusively with The Globe and Mail. Of those, roughly a third said they worked full-time. The food bank's client surveys show that the combination of elevated housing costs, rising grocery prices and stagnant wages has pushed a growing share of the workforce into a situation where income is simply not enough to cover basic expenses.

'We are not talking about people who are down on their luck in the traditional sense,' said Neil Hetherington, chief executive of Daily Bread. 'We are talking about nurses, construction workers, administrative staff, people doing every job that makes this city function. They are working and they still cannot afford to eat properly.'

The February figure represented a 12 per cent increase over the same month last year and more than triple the pre-pandemic baseline. The organization says it distributed the equivalent of more than 1.2 million meals, a figure that strains its logistics, volunteer networks and storage capacity.

Fernando Morales, 44, works two part-time retail jobs and visited a Daily Bread location in Scarborough for the first time in January. He said he had resisted coming for months, associating food bank use with failure. 'I kept thinking it would get better. Rent went up again in January and I did the math and I just couldn't do it anymore,' he said. 'The shame is something you have to push past.'

Researchers who study food insecurity say the record numbers reflect structural problems that short-term charity cannot solve. Valerie Tarasuk, a professor at the University of Toronto who leads the PROOF food insecurity research program, said the root cause was insufficient income relative to the cost of necessities. 'Food banks are a response to poverty, not a solution to it,' she said. 'What drives these numbers is the gap between what people earn and what they need to pay for housing and food.'

City of Toronto data shows that average asking rents for a one-bedroom apartment remained above $2,200 a month in February, even as the rental market showed some signs of softening from its 2023 peak. The Ontario minimum wage of $17.20 an hour translates to roughly $2,600 a month before taxes for a full-time worker.

Federal opposition parties seized on the food bank data to criticize the government's handling of affordability, with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre calling the numbers 'a devastating indictment of eight years of Liberal economic management.' The government pointed to recent GST relief and enhanced Canada Child Benefit payments as steps taken to help lower-income households.

Hetherington said Daily Bread was not resourced to handle continued growth at the current pace and called on all levels of government to accelerate investments in affordable housing and to revisit income support levels. 'We will feed people. That is what we do,' he said. 'But we should not be the answer to this problem.'
